verdict_reasoning: EREG (epiregulin) is an EGF-family member synthesized as a single-pass type I transmembrane precursor (~170 aa) that is anchored at the cell surface before regulated ectodomain shedding (primarily by ADAM10/ADAM17) releases the soluble 46-aa mature form. The membrane-anchored precursor stage is real and mediates juxtacrine signaling, but it is transient — the dominant biological actor is the shed soluble ligand that signals through EGFR/ERBB4. This matches the 'transient TM precursor of a shed-ligand-dominant gene' pattern: the TM stage qualifies for contextual surface accessibility (dual_localization / transient precursor), but the protein is not a canonical stable surface receptor. Anti-EREG therapeutic programs typically target the shed/soluble pool. Pre-no checklist: no documented cancer ecto-pool beyond precursor; no lysosomal exocytosis; no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering beyond the precursor itself; tissue restriction is broad. The TM precursor is the surface story.
